concepts.name: Urban Mobility
concepts.definition: A field spanning transit, micromobility, autonomy, and city policy.

concepts.name: Mobility Layer Fragmentation
concepts.definition: The risk that each actor optimizes its own layer instead of the whole mobility system.

concepts.name: E-Bikes
concepts.definition: Low-cost electric bikes with little parking footprint that work well in dense corridors and short trips.

concepts.name: Autonomous Vehicles
concepts.definition: Driverless cars and shuttles whose promise is constrained by mapped domains, edge cases, road space, and sprawl.

concepts.name: Shared E-Scooters
concepts.definition: Shared micromobility vehicles whose economics and climate value depend on durability and trip context.

concepts.name: Swappable Batteries
concepts.definition: Battery systems in newer scooter fleets that help make payback periods more viable.

concepts.name: Walking Replacement Risk
concepts.definition: The concern that scooters may replace walking rather than car trips, weakening climate benefits.

concepts.name: Congestion Pricing
concepts.definition: A road-pricing policy described as producing fast, durable traffic reductions.

concepts.name: Reduced Car Dependence
concepts.definition: The author's chosen north star metric for judging mobility solutions.

concepts.name: 15-Minute City
concepts.definition: A land-use model where daily needs are within a short walk or ride.

concepts.name: Zoning Reform
concepts.definition: Changing zoning is implied as needed because past zoning separated jobs and housing.

concepts.name: Jobs-Housing Co-location
concepts.definition: Placing jobs and housing near each other, an assumption of the 15-minute city.

concepts.name: Robotaxi Services
concepts.definition: Driverless taxi services running in a few cities but limited by narrow, heavily mapped domains.

concepts.name: Autonomous Shuttles
concepts.definition: Fixed-route autonomous vehicles predicted to scale before general robotaxis.

concepts.name: Edge Cases
concepts.definition: Rare, weird street interactions that dominate autonomy safety risk.

concepts.name: Miles-per-Intervention
concepts.definition: An autonomy metric called misleading because rare tail events dominate risk.

concepts.name: Short Urban Car Trips
concepts.definition: Mostly sub-5 km urban car trips that e-bikes and transit can address.

concepts.name: Transit
concepts.definition: A capacity-focused mobility mode that can be extended by scooters and strengthened by investment.

concepts.name: Charging Infrastructure
concepts.definition: The hidden electrification bottleneck around where and how fast vehicles can plug in.

concepts.name: Curbside Charging
concepts.definition: Dense-neighborhood vehicle charging at the curb, described as basically unsolved.

concepts.name: First and Last Mile Transit
concepts.definition: Connections to transit stops that extend transit catchment and can pull trips off cars.

concepts.name: Context-Dependent Mode Shift
concepts.definition: The idea that scooter substitution differs by setting, replacing cars near transit and walking in walkable cores.

concepts.name: Bus Rapid Transit
concepts.definition: A bus system using dedicated lanes and signal priority to get much of light rail's benefit faster and cheaper.

concepts.name: Dedicated Bus Lanes
concepts.definition: Street lanes reserved for buses that improve transit and can deploy faster than rail.

concepts.name: Deployment Speed
concepts.definition: A criterion that weights benefits by how quickly an intervention can be delivered.
aliases: benefit per year of deployment time

concepts.name: Vehicle-to-Grid
concepts.definition: Using parked EVs as distributed storage, blocked by missing standards and incentives.

concepts.name: Geometry Problem
concepts.definition: The spatial inefficiency of moving people in single-occupant boxes regardless of driver.

concepts.name: Spatial Efficiency
concepts.definition: Moving more people through the same street space, favoring transit, bikes, and walking over cars.

concepts.name: Curb Management
concepts.definition: Governance of contested curb space used by delivery, ride-hail, scooters, buses, and parking.

concepts.name: Dynamic Curb Pricing
concepts.definition: Pricing curb use dynamically, predicted to emerge in major cities within a decade.

concepts.name: Equity
concepts.definition: The concern that pricing and premium mobility can entrench access gaps without safeguards.

concepts.name: Mobility Stack
concepts.definition: A strategy combining demand pricing, transit and bikes, constrained autonomy, and better curb and street data.

concepts.name: Street Data
concepts.definition: Data that enables dynamic curb pricing, coordinated mobility stacks, and reprogrammable streets.

concepts.name: Reprogrammable Street Space
concepts.definition: Streets treated as instrumented, re-allocatable, and priced rather than fixed concrete.

concepts.name: Induced Demand
concepts.definition: The tendency for added road capacity to fill with new traffic.

concepts.name: Evaporating Demand
concepts.definition: The effect where reducing car lanes can reduce total car traffic rather than merely displacing it.

concepts.name: Protected Bike Lanes
concepts.definition: Protected cycling lanes named as a boring but high-impact intervention.

concepts.name: Urban Logistics
concepts.definition: The freight and delivery side of mobility that the author identifies for future study.

concepts.name: Cargo Bikes and Micro-Hubs
concepts.definition: A potential underexplored answer for exploding urban logistics.

concepts.name: Pricing and Allocation
concepts.definition: The final framing that scarce street space should be priced and allocated rather than solved by vehicles.
aliases: price and allocate scarce street space

claims.text: The journal begins with urban mobility as a fragmented field where each actor optimizes its own layer.
E-bikes, not autonomous cars, are argued to be the real near-term revolution because of favorable economics and little parking footprint.
Shared e-scooters had terrible first-wave unit economics because vehicles broke within months.
Ruggedized scooters with swappable batteries make the scooter payback period look sane.
It is unclear whether e-scooters replace car trips or mostly replace walking.
Congestion pricing is identified as a high-leverage policy lever with real and fast demand response.
The author decides to use reduced car dependence as the north star metric rather than technology adoption.
The 15-minute city reframes mobility as a symptom of urban design rather than a vehicle problem.
The 15-minute city assumes jobs and housing can be co-located, but decades of zoning did the opposite.
Robotaxi services are running driverless in a few cities but still have narrow, heavily mapped operating domains.
Autonomous shuttles on fixed routes will scale before general robotaxis because constrained routes shrink edge cases.
Miles-per-intervention is misleading because rare autonomy edge cases dominate risk.
Most urban car trips are under 5 km, creating an underserved market for e-bikes and transit.
Charging infrastructure is the hidden bottleneck for electrification, more than battery chemistry.
Curbside charging in dense neighborhoods is basically unsolved.
The author revises scooter skepticism because scooters linked to transit stops extend catchment and pull trips off cars.
The January walking-replacement worry is contradicted by evidence that scooter impacts are mode-specific and context-dependent.
Bus rapid transit is underrated because dedicated lanes and signal priority deliver much of light rail's benefit faster and cheaper.
The author decides to weight solutions by benefit per year of deployment time.
Vehicle-to-grid could turn parked EVs into distributed storage but is blocked by missing standards and incentives.
Even a perfect self-driving car still takes road space and can encourage sprawl.
The geometry argument reframes mobility as fundamentally a spatial-efficiency problem.
Transit, bikes, and walking win because they move more people through the same street space, while cars lose on geometry.
Curb management may be the most contested and least digitized part of the system.
Dynamic curb pricing will emerge within a decade in major cities as the necessary data becomes available.
Congestion pricing and premium mobility can entrench access gaps without rebates or transit investment.
The winning strategy is a stack of pricing, transit and bikes, constrained autonomy, and better curb and street data.
Cities making progress treat streets as software: instrumented, re-allocatable, and priced.
Induced demand is a strong argument against building out of congestion because added road capacity fills with new traffic.
Reducing car lanes can reduce total car traffic rather than merely displacing it.
The author now sees bus lanes, protected bike lanes, congestion pricing, and curb pricing as higher-impact than flashy technologies.
The tech that matters is pricing and allocation, not vehicles.
Freight and delivery remain an open question because the reading has been passenger-centric while urban logistics is exploding.
Cargo bikes plus micro-hubs look like an underexplored answer for urban logistics.
The author's model shifts from which vehicle wins to how scarce street space should be priced and allocated.

relations.rationale: The field is described as fragmented across mobility layers.
E-bikes are contrasted with autonomous cars as the near-term revolution and later with flashy vehicle tech.
Swappable batteries help newer scooter fleets achieve saner payback periods.
The author questions and later narrows the risk that scooters replace walking.
Congestion pricing reduces traffic and fits the later north-star metric.
It reframes mobility as a symptom of urban design.
The concept assumes jobs and housing can be co-located.
Past zoning did the opposite of co-location, making retrofit necessary.
Changing zoning is implied as a route to co-locating jobs and housing.
Robotaxis are a driverless service form of autonomous vehicles.
Fixed-route shuttles are predicted to scale before general robotaxis.
Constrained routes shrink the long tail of edge cases.
The metric misses rare tail risks that dominate safety.
Sub-5 km trips are where e-bikes are competitive.
Sub-5 km trips are also where transit can compete.
Curbside charging is a specific unsolved infrastructure problem.
Vehicle-to-grid is blocked by charging standards and related infrastructure conditions.
Scooters connected to transit stops extend transit catchment.
First and last mile access is defined around connecting to transit stops.
The later note narrows the earlier worry by making substitution context-specific.
BRT's benefit comes from dedicated lanes plus signal priority.
A bus lane next year can outperform slower perfect rail, and bus lanes are later high-impact.
After choosing car dependence as the metric, the author adds deployment time as a weighting factor.
Self-driving cars still occupy inefficient road space, challenging autonomy hype.
The geometry argument becomes a broader spatial-efficiency model.
Contested curb governance leads to a pricing approach for curb space.
The prediction depends on curb data becoming available.
Pricing can entrench access gaps without rebates or transit investment.
Demand pricing is one layer of the proposed stack.
Transit is included in the stack for capacity.
Autonomy remains only where constrained rather than as the whole solution.
Better curb and street data coordinates the stack.
Treating streets as software depends on instrumentation and data.
The note says induced demand cuts both ways, including traffic evaporation when lanes are removed.
Evaporating demand extends induced-demand logic to reduced road capacity.
Protected bike lanes are named among the high-impact boring allocation interventions.
The author concludes pricing and allocation matter more than vehicles.
Cargo bikes plus micro-hubs are suggested for freight and delivery.
Reprogrammable streets are re-allocatable and priced, matching the final scarcity framing.
The final reflection says the vehicle question is downstream of geometry and pricing.
